LangGraph vs CrewAI vs AutoGen: Agent Framework Comparison | Lushbinary

AI & AutomationApril 7, 202615 min read

# LangGraph vs CrewAI vs AutoGen: AI Agent Framework Comparison for Production in 2026

Three frameworks dominate AI agent development in 2026. LangGraph offers graph-based production control. CrewAI enables fastest prototyping with role-based teams. AutoGen excels at collaborative reasoning on Azure. We compare architecture, benchmarks, MCP integration, and costs.

Lushbinary Team

AI & Cloud Solutions

Building AI agents in 2026 means choosing between three dominant frameworks: LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen. Each takes a fundamentally different approach to agent orchestration — graph-based workflows, role-based teams, and multi-agent conversations. The right choice depends on whether you're optimizing for production control, prototyping speed, or collaborative reasoning.

We've built production agent systems with all three frameworks at Lushbinary. This guide covers architecture deep-dives, benchmark comparisons, code examples, MCP integration patterns, and a decision framework to help you pick the right tool for your use case.

Whether you're building a customer support pipeline, a research automation system, or a multi-step reasoning engine, this comparison has the technical depth you need to make an informed decision.

## 📋 Table of Contents

1. 1.Why Agent Frameworks Matter in 2026
2. 2.LangGraph: Graph-Based Production Workflows
3. 3.CrewAI: Role-Based Team Orchestration
4. 4.AutoGen: Multi-Agent Conversations
5. 5.Architecture Comparison: Graphs vs Roles vs Conversations
6. 6.Head-to-Head Benchmark Comparison
7. 7.MCP Integration Across Frameworks
8. 8.When to Use Which Framework
9. 9.Migration & Interoperability
10. 10.Why Lushbinary for AI Agent Development

## 1Why Agent Frameworks Matter in 2026

Raw LLM API calls aren't enough for production AI agents. You need state management, error recovery, tool orchestration, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and observability. That's what agent frameworks provide — the infrastructure layer between your application logic and the LLM.

In 2024, most teams hand-rolled agent loops with basic prompt-chaining. By 2026, the complexity of production agent systems has made frameworks essential. Multi-step workflows, parallel tool execution, conditional branching, and persistent memory all require structured orchestration that ad-hoc code can't reliably deliver.

📊

#### Graph-Based (LangGraph)

Define agent workflows as directed graphs with nodes and edges. Maximum control over execution flow, branching, and state transitions.

👥

#### Role-Based (CrewAI)

Define agents with roles, backstories, and goals. Agents collaborate as a crew with delegated tasks and shared context.

💬

#### Conversational (AutoGen)

Agents communicate through multi-turn conversations, debating and refining outputs collaboratively. Best for reasoning-heavy tasks.

Key insight

The framework you choose determines your ceiling. Graph-based orchestration (LangGraph) scales better than conversational patterns (AutoGen) for complex workflows because you can explicitly define execution paths. Conversational patterns excel at open-ended reasoning but become unpredictable as task complexity grows.

## 2LangGraph: Graph-Based Production Workflows

LangGraph, built by the LangChain team, models agent workflows as stateful directed graphs. Each node is a function (an LLM call, a tool invocation, a conditional check), and edges define the execution flow between them. This gives you explicit, debuggable control over every step your agent takes.

### Core Capabilities

- Stateful execution: Persistent state flows through the graph. Each node can read and write to a shared state object, enabling complex multi-step workflows with memory.
- Streaming: Native token-level and node-level streaming. You can stream partial results from any node as the graph executes, critical for real-time UIs.
- Checkpointing: Save and restore graph execution state at any point. Enables human-in-the-loop approval gates, error recovery, and long-running workflows that survive restarts.
- Time-travel debugging: Via LangSmith, you can replay any execution step-by-step, inspect state at each node, and identify exactly where things went wrong.
- Conditional branching: Route execution based on state, LLM output, or custom logic. Build complex decision trees without spaghetti code.

### LangGraph Code Example

// LangGraph — stateful agent with tool use and checkpointing

import { StateGraph, MessagesAnnotation } from "@langchain/langgraph";

import { ToolNode } from "@langchain/langgraph/prebuilt";

import { ChatOpenAI } from "@langchain/openai";

const model = new ChatOpenAI({ model: "gpt-4o" });

const tools = [searchTool, calculatorTool];

const toolNode = new ToolNode(tools);

const graph = new StateGraph(MessagesAnnotation)

.addNode("agent", async (state) => {

const response = await model.bindTools(tools).invoke(state.messages);

return { messages: [response] };

})

.addNode("tools", toolNode)

.addEdge("__start__", "agent")

.addConditionalEdges("agent", shouldContinue)

.addEdge("tools", "agent")

.compile({ checkpointer: sqliteCheckpointer });

// Execute with streaming and state persistence

for await (const event of graph.stream(input, { configurable: { thread_id: "1" } })) {

console.log(event);

}

### Where LangGraph Excels

- Complex multi-step workflows with conditional logic and parallel branches
- Production systems that need checkpointing, error recovery, and human-in-the-loop gates
- Teams that need deep observability via LangSmith for debugging and monitoring
- Long-running agent tasks that must survive process restarts

### Where LangGraph Falls Short

- Steeper learning curve — you need to think in graphs, not sequential scripts
- More boilerplate for simple use cases compared to CrewAI's declarative DSL
- LangSmith dependency for full observability (paid service)

## 3CrewAI: Role-Based Team Orchestration

CrewAI takes a fundamentally different approach: instead of defining graphs, you define agents with human-like roles, backstories, and goals. These agents form a "crew" that collaborates on tasks, delegating work and sharing context automatically. It's the fastest path from idea to working multi-agent prototype.

### Core Capabilities

- Role-based DSL: Define agents with role, backstory, and goal fields. The framework uses these to shape agent behavior and inter-agent communication.
- Crew collaboration: Agents work together as a crew with sequential or hierarchical task execution. Agents can delegate sub-tasks to other crew members.
- Fastest prototyping: A working multi-agent system in under 50 lines of code. CrewAI's declarative syntax minimizes boilerplate.
- Built-in tool integration: Extensive library of pre-built tools for web search, file operations, and API calls.
- Memory system: Short-term, long-term, and entity memory for agents to maintain context across tasks.

### CrewAI Code Example

# CrewAI — role-based multi-agent research crew

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

researcher = Agent(

role="Senior Research Analyst",

backstory="You are an expert at finding and synthesizing technical information.",

goal="Find comprehensive data on the given topic",

tools=[search_tool, scrape_tool],

llm="gpt-4o"

)

writer = Agent(

role="Technical Writer",

backstory="You write clear, accurate technical content.",

goal="Create a well-structured report from research findings",

llm="gpt-4o"

)

crew = Crew(

agents=[researcher, writer],

tasks=[research_task, writing_task],

process=Process.sequential

)

result = crew.kickoff()

### Where CrewAI Falls Short

- Limited observability: No equivalent to LangSmith for tracing and debugging agent execution. You're largely relying on logs.
- Less production control: The declarative DSL abstracts away execution details, making it harder to implement fine-grained error handling and recovery.
- Scaling challenges: Role-based orchestration works well for 2-5 agents but becomes harder to manage with larger agent teams.

## 4AutoGen: Multi-Agent Conversations

AutoGen, developed by Microsoft, takes the conversational approach to multi-agent systems. Agents communicate through structured multi-turn conversations, debating, critiquing, and refining each other's outputs. It's the most natural fit for tasks that benefit from collaborative reasoning — code review, research synthesis, and complex analysis.

### Core Capabilities

- Multi-agent debates: Agents engage in structured conversations where they challenge, validate, and build on each other's reasoning. Produces higher-quality outputs for complex problems.
- Collaborative reasoning: Multiple agents with different perspectives work through problems together, catching errors that single-agent systems miss.
- Azure-native integration: Deep integration with Azure OpenAI Service, Azure identity management, and Microsoft compliance tooling. The natural choice for Azure-first teams.
- Flexible conversation patterns: Two-agent chat, group chat with speaker selection, nested conversations, and custom conversation flows.
- Human-in-the-loop: Built-in support for human proxy agents that can intervene in conversations at any point.

### AutoGen Code Example

# AutoGen — multi-agent collaborative code review

from autogen import AssistantAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

coder = AssistantAgent(

name="Coder",

system_message="You write clean, production-ready Python code.",

llm_config={"model": "gpt-4o"}

)

reviewer = AssistantAgent(

name="Reviewer",

system_message="You review code for bugs, security issues, and performance.",

llm_config={"model": "gpt-4o"}

)

user_proxy = UserProxyAgent(

name="User",

human_input_mode="TERMINATE",

code_execution_config={"work_dir": "output"}

)

group_chat = GroupChat(agents=[coder, reviewer, user_proxy], messages=[])

manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=group_chat)

user_proxy.initiate_chat(manager, message="Build a REST API for user management")

Cost warning

AutoGen's conversational pattern typically generates 20+ LLM calls per task as agents debate and refine outputs. This makes it significantly more expensive per task than LangGraph or CrewAI. Budget accordingly and set conversation turn limits in production.

### Where AutoGen Falls Short

- High LLM cost: 20+ calls per task adds up fast. Conversational patterns are inherently more expensive than structured orchestration.
- Unpredictable execution: Conversation flow is harder to control than graph-based or sequential execution. Agents can go off-track in long conversations.
- Azure bias: While it supports any OpenAI-compatible API, the best experience is on Azure. Teams on AWS or GCP may find the integration less seamless.

## 5Architecture Comparison: Graphs vs Roles vs Conversations

The architectural differences between these three frameworks aren't just academic — they determine how your agent system behaves in production, how easy it is to debug, and how well it scales.

| Dimension | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Orchestration model | Directed graph | Role-based crew | Multi-agent conversation |
| State management | Explicit graph state | Shared crew context | Conversation history |
| Execution flow | Deterministic edges | Sequential / hierarchical | Dynamic conversation |
| Debugging | LangSmith time-travel | Logging only | Conversation traces |
| Checkpointing | ✅ Built-in | ❌ Manual | ⚠️ Limited |
| Streaming | ✅ Native | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited |
| Error recovery | Graph-level retry | Task-level retry | Conversation restart |
| Learning curve | High (graph thinking) | Low (declarative DSL) | Medium (conversation design) |
| Scalability | Excellent | Good (2-5 agents) | Moderate (cost-limited) |
| LLM calls per task | 2-8 (structured) | 3-10 (sequential) | 20+ (conversational) |

The key architectural takeaway: LangGraph gives you the most control and the best production characteristics (checkpointing, streaming, deterministic execution). CrewAI gives you the fastest path to a working prototype. AutoGen gives you the deepest collaborative reasoning but at the highest cost and with the least predictable execution.

### Scaling Characteristics

Graph-based orchestration scales linearly — adding a new node to a LangGraph workflow is O(1) in complexity. Role-based orchestration (CrewAI) scales well up to about 5 agents, after which inter-agent coordination overhead grows. Conversational patterns (AutoGen) scale poorly because each additional agent multiplies the number of conversation turns, and therefore LLM calls, needed to reach consensus. For workflows with 10+ steps or 5+ agents, LangGraph is the clear winner.

## 6Head-to-Head Benchmark Comparison

We benchmarked all three frameworks on identical tasks using GPT-4o as the underlying model. This isolates the framework's orchestration quality from the model's capabilities.

| Metric | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Setup time (hello world) | ~15 min | ~5 min | ~10 min |
| Multi-step task accuracy | 94% | 87% | 91% |
| Avg. LLM calls per task | 4.2 | 6.1 | 22.7 |
| Avg. cost per task (GPT-4o) | $0.08 | $0.12 | $0.45 |
| Error recovery rate | 96% | 72% | 68% |
| Streaming latency (TTFB) | 180ms | 1.2s | 2.8s |
| Checkpoint/resume | ✅ Native | ❌ Manual | ⚠️ Partial |
| Production observability | ✅ LangSmith | ⚠️ Basic logs | ⚠️ Conversation logs |

Benchmark context

Tasks included: multi-step research with web search, code generation with testing, document analysis with structured output, and customer support ticket routing. All tests used GPT-4o with identical system prompts where applicable. AutoGen's higher accuracy on reasoning tasks comes at 5-6x the cost of LangGraph due to the conversational overhead.

## 7MCP Integration Across Frameworks

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has become the standard for connecting AI agents to external tools and data sources in 2026. All three frameworks now support MCP, but the integration depth varies.

| MCP Feature | LangGraph | CrewAI | AutoGen |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MCP tool servers | ✅ As graph nodes | ✅ As agent tools | ✅ As conversation tools |
| MCP resource access | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| MCP prompts | ✅ | ⚠️ Partial | ⚠️ Partial |
| Dynamic tool discovery | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Limited |
| Multi-server support | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Streaming from MCP | ✅ Native | ❌ | ❌ |

### LangGraph MCP Integration Example

// LangGraph — MCP tool server as a graph node

import { MCPToolkit } from "@langchain/mcp";

import { StateGraph } from "@langchain/langgraph";

const mcpToolkit = new MCPToolkit({

servers: [

{ name: "database", url: "http://localhost:3001/mcp" },

{ name: "search", url: "http://localhost:3002/mcp" },

]

});

const tools = await mcpToolkit.getTools();

// Tools are automatically available as graph nodes

const graph = new StateGraph(MessagesAnnotation)

.addNode("agent", agentNode)

.addNode("mcp_tools", new ToolNode(tools))

.compile();

LangGraph's MCP integration is the deepest because MCP tools become first-class graph nodes with full streaming support. CrewAI and AutoGen treat MCP tools as callable functions, which works but doesn't leverage MCP's streaming capabilities. For teams building tool-heavy agent systems, LangGraph's MCP integration is a significant advantage.

## 8When to Use Which Framework

Here's the decision framework we use at Lushbinary when recommending agent frameworks to clients:

🏭

#### Choose LangGraph

- ✓ Production-critical agent systems
- ✓ Complex multi-step workflows with branching
- ✓ Need checkpointing and error recovery
- ✓ Require deep observability (LangSmith)
- ✓ Streaming responses to end users
- ✓ Long-running tasks that must survive restarts

🚀

#### Choose CrewAI

- ✓ Fast prototyping and MVPs
- ✓ Team-based agent collaboration
- ✓ Simple sequential or hierarchical workflows
- ✓ Non-technical stakeholders defining agents
- ✓ Internal tools and automation
- ✓ Proof-of-concept demos

☁️

#### Choose AutoGen

- ✓ Azure-first infrastructure
- ✓ Tasks requiring collaborative reasoning
- ✓ Code review and quality assurance
- ✓ Research synthesis and analysis
- ✓ Multi-perspective decision making
- ✓ Microsoft ecosystem integration

Our recommendation

For most production use cases, start with LangGraph. Its graph-based architecture gives you the control and observability you need when things go wrong (and they will). Use CrewAI for rapid prototyping and internal tools where speed-to-demo matters more than production hardening. Use AutoGen when you're on Azure and need collaborative reasoning capabilities that justify the higher per-task cost.

## 9Migration & Interoperability

A common pattern we see: teams prototype with CrewAI, then migrate to LangGraph for production. Here's what that migration looks like and how to plan for interoperability between frameworks.

### CrewAI → LangGraph Migration

- Agent → Node mapping: Each CrewAI agent becomes a node in the LangGraph graph. The agent's role and backstory become the system prompt for that node's LLM call.
- Task → Edge mapping: CrewAI's sequential task flow maps directly to edges in the graph. Hierarchical flows require conditional edges.
- Tool migration: CrewAI tools and LangGraph tools both use the LangChain tool interface, so most tools transfer directly without modification.
- State refactoring: CrewAI's implicit shared context needs to be refactored into explicit LangGraph state annotations. This is the most labor-intensive part.

### Hybrid Architectures

You don't have to pick just one. Some of the most effective agent systems we've built use multiple frameworks:

- LangGraph + AutoGen: Use LangGraph as the orchestration backbone, with AutoGen-style multi-agent debates as a single node for tasks that benefit from collaborative reasoning.
- CrewAI for internal tools, LangGraph for customer-facing: Different reliability requirements justify different frameworks within the same organization.
- MCP as the bridge: All three frameworks support MCP, so you can share tool servers across frameworks. Build your tools once as MCP servers and use them from any framework.

## 10Why Lushbinary for AI Agent Development

We've built production agent systems with LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen across industries including fintech, healthcare, and e-commerce. Our team understands the tradeoffs between these frameworks at a deep architectural level — not just from reading docs, but from shipping real systems that handle thousands of agent executions daily.

- Framework selection & architecture: We assess your use case, team capabilities, and infrastructure to recommend the right framework (or combination) for your needs.
- Production agent pipelines: We design and build agent workflows with proper checkpointing, error recovery, observability, and cost controls from day one.
- MCP tool server development: We build custom MCP servers that connect your agents to internal databases, APIs, and business logic — reusable across any framework.
- Migration & optimization: Moving from prototype to production? We handle CrewAI-to-LangGraph migrations, cost optimization, and performance tuning.
- Observability & monitoring: We set up LangSmith dashboards, custom metrics, alerting, and cost tracking so you know exactly how your agents are performing.

🚀 Free consultation

Not sure which agent framework fits your use case? We offer a free 30-minute consultation to assess your requirements, recommend the right architecture, and outline an implementation plan. Book a call →

### ❓ Frequently Asked Questions

#### What is the best AI agent framework for production in 2026?

LangGraph is the best AI agent framework for production in 2026. Its graph-based workflow engine provides stateful execution, streaming, checkpointing, and time-travel debugging via LangSmith. It gives teams fine-grained control over agent behavior, making it ideal for complex, mission-critical AI agent deployments.

#### LangGraph vs CrewAI: which should I choose?

Choose LangGraph for production systems that need fine-grained control, stateful workflows, streaming, and observability via LangSmith. Choose CrewAI for fast prototyping and team-based agent orchestration where you want to define agents with roles, backstories, and goals using a simple DSL. CrewAI gets you to a working prototype faster; LangGraph scales better in production.

#### Does AutoGen work well outside of Azure?

AutoGen supports any OpenAI-compatible API, but its deepest integrations are with Azure OpenAI Service. Teams running on Azure get native identity management, compliance tooling, and optimized inference endpoints. Outside Azure, AutoGen works but you lose some of the managed infrastructure benefits.

#### Do LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen support MCP in 2026?

Yes. All three frameworks support the Model Context Protocol (MCP) as of 2026. LangGraph integrates MCP tools as graph nodes. CrewAI supports MCP tool servers as agent tools. AutoGen agents can call MCP-compatible tool servers during multi-agent conversations.

#### How many LLM calls does AutoGen use per task?

AutoGen's multi-agent conversation pattern typically uses 20+ LLM calls per task because each agent generates independent responses, critiques, and refinements. This makes AutoGen powerful for collaborative reasoning but significantly more expensive per task than LangGraph or CrewAI.

NeuralRouting Team

# Best AI Gateway & LLM Router in 2026: Independent Comparison

The AI gateway market has consolidated fast. In early 2026, three events reshaped the landscape: Helicone was acquired by Mintlify (entering maintenance mode), LiteLLM suffered a supply chain attack that compromised thousands of deployments, and Portkey locked compliance features behind enterprise pricing. If you're evaluating gateways today, the decision matrix looks very different from six months ago.

This guide compares the six major options across pricing, features, latency, and operational complexity. No sponsored rankings — just engineering analysis.

---

## The Contenders

| Gateway | Language | Approach | Best For |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Portkey.ai | TypeScript | Managed SaaS, enterprise focus | Teams needing full observability stack |
| Helicone | Rust | Managed SaaS (maintenance mode) | Existing users; new users should evaluate alternatives |
| LiteLLM | Python | Self-hosted open source | Teams with DevOps capacity wanting full control |
| OpenRouter | Proprietary | Managed marketplace | Access to 400+ models with unified API |
| Vercel AI Gateway | TypeScript | Framework-native (Vercel ecosystem) | Next.js teams already on Vercel |
| NeuralRouting.io | Python | Managed SaaS with intelligent routing | Cost optimization with quality guarantees |

---

## Pricing Breakdown (Real Numbers)

Understanding the true cost of each gateway requires looking beyond the sticker price. Some charge per log, some per request, some take a markup on model costs.

### Portkey.ai

- Free: 10K logs/month, 3-day retention
- Production: $49/month (100K logs, 15-day retention)
- Enterprise: Custom pricing (required for SOC2, SSO, self-hosting)

Hidden costs: Guardrails, advanced analytics, and compliance are enterprise-only. You won't know the price until you talk to sales. Users on G2 report documentation gaps that increase onboarding time.

### Helicone

- Free: 10K requests/month
- Pro: $79/month (unlimited seats, 1-year retention)
- Team: $799/month (SOC2 + HIPAA compliance)

Current status: Acquired by Mintlify in March 2026. Only receiving security patches. No new features planned. The 16,000 organizations using Helicone need to plan their migration.

### LiteLLM

- Open Source: Free (requires self-hosting: Redis + PostgreSQL + your infra)
- Enterprise Basic: $250/month
- Enterprise Premium: $30,000/year

Hidden costs: Infrastructure ($200-$500/month for Redis + PostgreSQL + compute), 2-4 weeks DevOps setup time, ongoing maintenance burden. The March 2026 supply chain attack (versions 1.82.7-1.82.8 compromised with credential-stealing malware) adds a trust cost that's hard to quantify.

### OpenRouter

- Free: No gateway fee
- Markup: 5.5% on all model costs

At scale: On $10K/month in model costs, you're paying $550/month in markup. At $50K/month, that's $2,750. No routing intelligence, no caching, no quality guarantees. Pure pass-through.

### Vercel AI Gateway

- Free: Included with Vercel Pro ($20/month)
- Features: Rate limiting, caching, basic analytics

Limitations: Tightly coupled to Vercel ecosystem. Limited provider support compared to dedicated gateways. No intelligent routing or cost optimization.

### NeuralRouting.io

- Free: 5K credits/month
- Starter: $29/month (50K credits)
- Growth: $89/month (200K credits)
- Business: $349/month (1M credits)

What's included at every tier: Intelligent model routing, semantic caching, quality validation via Shadow Engine, prompt security shield, spend analytics. No features locked behind enterprise pricing.

---

## Feature Matrix

| Feature | Portkey | Helicone | LiteLLM | OpenRouter | Vercel AI | NeuralRouting |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Intelligent routing | Basic fallback | Basic (PeakEWMA) | Basic fallback | None | None | Per-query complexity routing |
| Semantic caching | Production+ only | Edge caching | No | Prompt caching | Basic | All tiers |
| Quality validation | No | No | No | No | No | Shadow Engine |
| Prompt security | Guardrails (Enterprise) | Rate limiting only | Enterprise only | No | Rate limiting | All tiers |
| Cost optimization | Manual model selection | No | Manual | No | No | Automatic (Model Cascading) |
| Self-healing routing | No | No | No | No | No | Confidence Matrix |
| Models supported | 250+ | 100+ | 100+ | 400+ | 20+ | 5 (growing) |
| OpenAI SDK compatible | Yes | Yes (proxy) | Yes | Yes | Yes (AI SDK) | Yes |
| Observability | Full | Full | Basic | Basic | Basic | Dashboard + API |
| Active development | Yes | No (maintenance) | Yes (recovering) | Yes | Yes | Yes |

---

## Latency Comparison

Latency is critical for production AI applications. Every millisecond of gateway overhead compounds across thousands of daily requests.

- Portkey: Not publicly disclosed. Enterprise users report reasonable performance but no published benchmarks.
- Helicone: ~8ms P50, ~50ms P95 gateway overhead. Rust-based, historically fast.
- LiteLLM: ~8ms per request (self-hosted, depends on your infrastructure).
- OpenRouter: Variable. Pass-through to providers with marketplace overhead.
- Vercel AI Gateway: Low overhead when deployed on Vercel edge network.
- NeuralRouting: ~118ms average including routing classification. The local heuristic classifier adds < 1ms; the rest is provider response time.

---

## The Routing Intelligence Gap

This is where the market diverges most sharply. Most gateways are proxies — they forward your request to the model you specified, add logging, and return the response. They don't decide which model to use.

NeuralRouting's Model Cascading is fundamentally different:

1. Classify: A zero-cost local classifier analyzes prompt complexity in < 1ms
2. Route: Simple tasks → Llama 3 (60x cheaper). Complex tasks → GPT-4o
3. Validate: The Shadow Engine compares economy responses against premium quality in the background
4. Learn: The Confidence Matrix tracks quality per (task_type, model) pair and automatically adjusts routing thresholds

This closed-loop system means the router gets smarter over time. No other gateway in this comparison offers this capability publicly.

---

## Who Should Use What

Choose Portkey if: You need full observability, your team has budget for enterprise pricing, and you want a mature managed platform.

Choose LiteLLM if: You have DevOps capacity, need self-hosting for data sovereignty, and can maintain the infrastructure long-term. Verify supply chain security carefully.

Choose OpenRouter if: You need access to 400+ models and don't care about cost optimization or intelligent routing.

Choose Vercel AI Gateway if: You're already on Vercel and need basic gateway features without adding another vendor.

Choose NeuralRouting if: Cost optimization is your primary goal, you want automatic routing intelligence without manual model selection, and you value quality guarantees backed by continuous validation.

Avoid Helicone for new projects: It's in maintenance mode with no roadmap.

---

## Conclusion

The AI gateway market in 2026 rewards different choices depending on your priorities. For pure observability, Portkey leads. For model access breadth, OpenRouter wins. For self-hosting control, LiteLLM (with security caveats) is the option.

But if you're optimizing for cost with quality guarantees — which is what most production teams actually need — the Model Cascading + Shadow Engine + Confidence Matrix combination in NeuralRouting is unmatched. No other gateway automatically routes by complexity, validates quality in real-time, and self-improves from production data.

The Model Tax is real. The question is whether you'll keep paying it.

# RelayPlane vs LiteLLM vs Helicone vs Bifrost: The LLM Gateway Comparison for 2026

Matt Turley·March 11, 2026·6 min read

LLM infrastructure has quietly become one of the messier parts of a production AI stack. You started with a single API key and a fetch call. Now you've got four providers, no idea which request cost what, and a bill that surprises you every month. The answer is a gateway layer, but which one?

Four tools come up constantly in this space: LiteLLM, Helicone, Bifrost, and RelayPlane. They are not interchangeable. They solve different problems, make different tradeoffs, and fit different stacks. Here is an honest breakdown.

---

## Quick Comparison

| RelayPlane | LiteLLM | Helicone | Bifrost |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Setup | `npm install @relayplane/proxy`, 3 lines of code, runs in seconds | `pip install litellm[proxy]`, Docker + Postgres for full features | Sign up for hosted service, add API headers | `npx @maximhq/bifrost`, Go binary |
| Language / Runtime | Node.js, npm-native | Python | Hosted SaaS (any language via headers) | Go (distributed via npx) |
| Request routing | Yes, complexity + cascade + mode-based, 11 providers | Yes, 100+ providers | No, observability only | Yes, adaptive load balancing |
| Cost tracking | Per-request, built in, no database required | Yes, requires Postgres for full tracking | Yes, per-request on hosted dashboard | Partial |
| Open source | Yes (github.com/RelayPlane/proxy) | Yes | No (cloud product, some OSS components) | Yes |

---

## When to Use Each

LiteLLM is the right call if your team runs Python and needs access to the full universe of models. With 100+ provider integrations, it is the most comprehensive option out there. The tradeoff: getting the interesting features (spend tracking, team management, virtual keys) means standing up a Postgres database and running Docker. For a Python ML team that already lives in that world, it is a natural fit. For a Node.js shop, adding Python infrastructure for a proxy layer is a real operational burden.

Helicone is for teams that want observability on their LLM calls without changing how those calls are made. You wrap your existing API key, point your base URL at Helicone, and you get a dashboard showing latency, cost, error rates, and user sessions. It is genuinely useful for debugging and cost analysis. The limitation is that it is not a router. Helicone does not route traffic between providers, enforce budgets, or do anything when a provider goes down. If you need those things, Helicone alone will not get you there.

Bifrost makes a specific bet: raw throughput above everything else. Built in Go, it claims sub-100 microsecond overhead at high request volumes. If you are at a scale where gateway latency shows up in your tail percentiles and you need cluster mode and horizontal scaling, Bifrost is worth evaluating. For most teams running a few thousand requests per day, you will not feel the difference, and Bifrost is closed-source on the hosted side with a smaller community than LiteLLM.

RelayPlane is the answer if you are working in Node.js and you want cost intelligence built in from day one. No Docker, no database, no external service to sign up for. Three lines of code and you have a running proxy with per-request cost tracking, complexity-based routing across 11 providers, and budget enforcement that actually does something (block, warn, or downgrade the request) when you hit your limit.

---

## What Makes RelayPlane Different

The pitch is simple: you should not need infrastructure to get started with an LLM gateway.

Three lines. No seriously.

```
npm install -g @relayplane/proxy
relayplane init
relayplane start
```

That is a working proxy with cost tracking and routing. No Docker Compose. No database migrations. You install a package, run two commands, and you're live.

```
npm install @relayplane/proxy
```

That is the entire install step.

Cost tracking per request, not per month. Most teams discover their LLM spend problem at billing time. By then it is too late to know which workflow caused the spike or which agent went off the rails. RelayPlane tracks input tokens, output tokens, and computed cost for every request. It handles Anthropic prompt cache read savings and write costs separately, so the numbers you see are accurate. You can set daily or per-request budget limits, and when something hits the limit, the proxy does not just log a warning, it can block the request or downgrade it to a cheaper model automatically.

Routing that maps task complexity to model cost. The routing config is explicit, not magic. You define what counts as a "simple" task and what counts as "complex," then map those levels to models. A simple text classification call goes to a fast, cheap model. A detailed code review goes to a capable one. You decide the rules; the proxy enforces them consistently across every request without you having to put that logic in your application code.

npm-installable, open source. The package is `@relayplane/proxy` on npm. The source is at github.com/RelayPlane/proxy. No vendor lock-in, no account required to run it locally.

---

## Switching from LiteLLM (or Others) to RelayPlane

If you are on LiteLLM and want to try RelayPlane, the migration is straightforward because both expose an OpenAI-compatible API. Your application code stays the same. You change one base URL.

Before (LiteLLM):

```
const OpenAI = require('openai');

const client = new OpenAI({
  apiKey: process.env.LITELLM_API_KEY,
  baseURL: 'http://localhost:4000',  // LiteLLM proxy
});

const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: 'gpt-4o',
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Hello' }],
});
```

After (RelayPlane):

```
# Setup (once)
npm install -g @relayplane/proxy
relayplane init    # walks you through provider keys
relayplane start   # proxy runs on localhost:4100
```

```
// Application code (same as before, just point to the proxy)
const OpenAI = require('openai');

const client = new OpenAI({
  apiKey: process.env.RELAYPLANE_API_KEY,
  baseURL: 'http://localhost:4100',  // RelayPlane proxy
});

const response = await client.chat.completions.create({
  model: 'gpt-4o',  // same model names work
  messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Hello' }],
});
```

The only change in your application code is the port number. The proxy handles the translation to whichever provider you have configured, and every request comes back with cost metadata attached.

Coming from Helicone is even simpler: Helicone requires no proxy, just header injection. RelayPlane also works as a drop-in baseURL replacement. You remove the Helicone headers, update the base URL to`http://localhost:4100`, and you go from observability to routing plus observability.

---

## Bottom Line

If you are in a Python shop and need the broadest model coverage possible, LiteLLM is the established choice. Accept the infrastructure overhead; it comes with the territory.

If you want cost visibility without routing, Helicone is a clean hosted option. Just know what it is: a dashboard, not a gateway. It will not save you from a provider outage or route traffic intelligently.

If you are processing serious request volume and latency at the gateway layer is a measurable problem, Bifrost is worth benchmarking.

If you are building in Node.js and you want a proxy that installs in thirty seconds, costs nothing to run locally, and gives you per-request cost tracking and intelligent routing out of the box, RelayPlane is the practical choice for 2026. Three lines of code. No Docker. Real cost data. That is the pitch, and for most Node.js teams, it holds up.

```
npm install @relayplane/proxy
```

Start there.

---

RelayPlane is open source. Source: github.com/RelayPlane/proxy. Package: `@relayplane/proxy` v1.8.10 on npm. Supports 11 providers. Last verified: 2026-03-11.

# The Complete Guide to LLM Routing: 5 AI Gateways Transforming Production AI Infrastructure

15 min read

Feb 9, 2026

--

Share

## TL;DR

LLM routing has become critical infrastructure for production AI applications in 2025. This comprehensive guide examines five leading AI gateways: Bifrost, Cloudflare AI Gateway, Vercel AI Gateway, Kong AI Gateway, and LiteLLM. We explore their architectures, routing strategies, and practical implementation patterns. For teams building reliable AI agents at scale, we demonstrate how Bifrost integrates with Maxim’s AI evaluation platform to transform LLM routing from infrastructure management into a strategic quality optimization lever.

## The LLM Infrastructure Challenge

Modern AI applications face an increasingly complex provider landscape. OpenAI releases new models monthly. Anthropic’s Claude dominates reasoning tasks. AWS Bedrock provides enterprise compliance. Google’s Gemini excels at multimodal processing.

Each provider implements different authentication schemes, API formats, rate limits, and pricing structures. Engineering teams spend weeks building abstraction layers instead of shipping features. Worse, when a single provider experiences downtime, entire applications fail.

This fragmentation creates three critical problems:

Integration complexity: Every new model requires custom integration code, testing, and maintenance. Teams build and maintain provider-specific wrappers that duplicate authentication, error handling, and retry logic.

Reliability risks: Single-provider dependencies create catastrophic failure modes. Without intelligent routing and fallback mechanisms, one API outage cascades through your entire system.

Cost optimization blindness: Without unified observability across providers, teams cannot answer fundamental questions: Which model delivers the best quality-to-cost ratio for specific use cases? Where are we overspending on capability we don’t need?

LLM gateways solve these challenges by providing unified interfaces, intelligent routing, and production-grade reliability infrastructure. But choosing the right gateway requires understanding architectural tradeoffs and operational implications.

## Understanding LLM Routing Strategies

Before evaluating specific platforms, understanding routing strategies helps frame the decision.

## Basic Load Balancing

The simplest routing distributes requests across multiple API keys or regional endpoints for the same model. This prevents rate limit throttling and improves availability but doesn’t optimize for cost or quality.

Most gateways support round-robin, least-connections, or random distribution. These strategies work well for homogeneous deployments where all endpoints provide identical capabilities.

## Intelligent Failover

Production systems require automatic failover when providers experience outages or degraded performance. Intelligent failover monitors endpoint health, tracks error rates, and automatically routes traffic to healthy providers.

The challenge lies in distinguishing temporary failures (retry after backoff) from sustained outages (failover immediately). Sophisticated gateways implement circuit breaker patterns that prevent cascading failures while minimizing latency during provider recovery.

## Cost-Based Routing

Different models have dramatically different pricing. GPT-4 Turbo costs significantly more than GPT-3.5 Turbo, while Claude Haiku offers another price point entirely.

Cost-based routing analyzes request characteristics and routes to the most economical model that meets quality requirements. Simple queries go to cheaper models; complex reasoning tasks justify premium pricing.

## Semantic Routing

The most sophisticated routing strategy analyzes prompt semantics and routes to models optimized for specific task types. A customer support query might route to a model fine-tuned for conversational empathy, while code generation requests target models trained on programming languages.

Semantic routing requires embedding models to vectorize prompts, similarity matching against model descriptions, and threshold-based routing decisions. The computational overhead is minimal compared to the quality and cost benefits.

## Performance-Based Routing

Latency matters for user-facing applications. Performance-based routing tracks response times across providers and models, routing latency-sensitive requests to fastest endpoints while batching background tasks to more economical but slower options.

This strategy requires real-time performance monitoring and dynamic routing tables that adapt as provider performance changes throughout the day.

## Comparative Analysis: Five Leading AI Gateways

## Bifrost: High-Performance Gateway With Integrated Evaluation

Bifrost is Maxim AI’s high-performance AI gateway designed specifically for teams shipping reliable AI agents at scale. Unlike standalone gateways that focus purely on infrastructure, Bifrost integrates with Maxim’s end-to-end evaluation platform, transforming routing from operational necessity into strategic quality lever.

Architecture and Performance

Bifrost delivers sub-10ms overhead through a lightweight proxy architecture written in Go. The gateway sits between your application and 12+ providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, Cohere, Mistral, Groq, and Ollama for local inference.

The unified interface provides OpenAI-compatible endpoints, making Bifrost a true drop-in replacement. Change one line of code from`https://api.openai.com` to your Bifrost endpoint, and you immediately gain multi-provider access with zero refactoring.

Intelligent Routing and Reliability

Automatic fallbacks ensure zero downtime when providers experience outages. Bifrost monitors endpoint health in real-time, implementing circuit breaker patterns that prevent cascading failures. When OpenAI returns 503 errors, Bifrost automatically retries with exponential backoff before failing over to Anthropic or other configured providers.

Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple API keys and regional endpoints, preventing rate limit throttling while optimizing for latency. The routing engine supports request-level provider selection, enabling applications to specify cost/performance tradeoffs dynamically based on use case.

Semantic Caching for Cost Optimization

Semantic caching represents Bifrost’s most innovative cost optimization feature. Unlike exact-match caching that only works for identical queries, semantic caching uses embedding-based similarity matching to identify semantically equivalent requests.

When a user asks “What’s the weather today?”, Bifrost generates an embedding vector. Later queries like “How’s the weather right now?” or “Tell me today’s weather” produce similar embeddings, triggering cache hits even though the exact text differs.

This semantic understanding reduces costs by up to 80% and latency by 90% for applications with common query patterns. Customer support chatbots, documentation assistants, and FAQ systems benefit enormously from semantic caching.

The caching layer integrates with Redis or in-memory stores, providing configurable TTLs and similarity thresholds. Teams can tune aggressiveness based on their quality-cost tradeoff preferences.

Model Context Protocol (MCP) Integration

MCP support enables AI models to access external tools like filesystems, databases, and web search APIs. This capability is critical for building production AI agents that need real-world capabilities beyond text generation.

Bifrost’s MCP integration allows agents to call APIs, query databases, search documentation, and execute code without custom tool-calling implementations. The gateway handles tool routing, authentication, and response formatting, letting developers focus on agent behavior rather than infrastructure plumbing.

Enterprise Governance and Security

Production deployments require robust governance. Bifrost provides hierarchical budget management with virtual keys, team-level controls, and customer-specific spending limits. Engineering teams can provision API access without exposing raw provider credentials.

SSO integration with Google and GitHub simplifies user management for enterprise deployments. HashiCorp Vault support enables secure API key storage and rotation, meeting compliance requirements for regulated industries.

The observability suite includes native Prometheus metrics, distributed tracing, and comprehensive logging. Teams gain visibility into request volumes, error rates, latency distributions, and cost attribution across providers and models.

Integration With Maxim’s Evaluation Platform

Bifrost’s differentiation comes from deep integration with Maxim’s AI evaluation platform. While other gateways stop at routing infrastructure, Bifrost enables continuous quality optimization through evaluation workflows.

Teams can A/B test routing strategies, measure quality metrics across different models, and optimize provider selection based on production data. The experimentation platform lets product managers and AI engineers collaborate on testing prompt variations, model comparisons, and cost-quality tradeoffs without writing code.

For example, Comm100 uses Bifrost with Maxim to evaluate whether GPT-4 provides sufficient quality improvement over GPT-3.5 to justify the 10x cost increase for customer support queries. Agent simulation generates hundreds of test conversations, routing through different models, and measuring resolution rates, customer satisfaction, and response quality.

This integration transforms routing from reactive infrastructure management into proactive quality optimization. Instead of asking “Is our gateway working?”, teams ask “Which routing strategy maximizes quality within our budget constraints?”

Mindtickle’s experience demonstrates this approach. They use Bifrost to route between multiple providers while Maxim’s observability platform tracks quality metrics in production. When evaluation scores drop, automated alerts trigger investigations before user impact escalates.

Deployment Flexibility

Bifrost supports multiple deployment models. Self-hosted deployments give teams complete control over data residency and security. Cloud deployments through Maxim provide managed infrastructure with SLAs. Hybrid models support on-premise inference for sensitive data while routing public queries to cloud providers.

The zero-config startup experience means developers can begin using Bifrost in minutes. Dynamic provider configuration through web UI, API-driven management, or declarative files provides flexibility for different operational models.

When to Choose Bifrost

Select Bifrost when you need high-performance routing with integrated quality evaluation. Teams shipping production AI agents benefit from the unified platform approach where infrastructure and measurement work together. The deep integration with Maxim’s evaluation capabilities makes Bifrost ideal for organizations treating AI quality as a competitive advantage, not just operational necessity.

## Cloudflare AI Gateway: Global Edge Infrastructure

Cloudflare AI Gateway leverages Cloudflare’s global edge network to provide low-latency LLM access with built-in caching and rate limiting. Running on infrastructure that handles 20% of Internet traffic, Cloudflare brings proven reliability and global distribution to AI workloads.

Core Architecture

The gateway runs at Cloudflare’s edge, reducing latency by routing requests to geographically optimal endpoints. This edge deployment provides automatic global scaling without managing regional infrastructure.

Cloudflare supports 20+ AI providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI, Groq, Perplexity, and Cloudflare’s own Workers AI. The unified API accepts OpenAI-compatible requests, automatically translating to provider-specific formats.

Caching and Performance Optimization

Response caching at the edge reduces latency by up to 90% for repeated queries. The caching layer stores responses at Cloudflare’s global edge locations, serving subsequent identical requests without hitting upstream providers. This dramatically reduces both latency and costs for applications with repetitive query patterns.

Rate limiting controls protect against abuse and manage spending. Teams can set limits per user, application, or time window using sliding or fixed window techniques. This granular control prevents runaway costs from unexpected traffic spikes.

Dynamic Routing and A/B Testing

Dynamic routing enables sophisticated request distribution without code changes. Visual routing rules route requests based on user segments, geography, content analysis, or A/B testing requirements.

This capability supports gradual model rollouts. When testing a new model version, route 10% of traffic to the new model while monitoring quality and performance metrics. Increase the percentage as confidence grows.

Unified Billing and Key Management

Cloudflare offers unified billing across all supported providers. Instead of managing separate accounts and invoices for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, teams pay Cloudflare based on usage at list pricing with minimal transaction fees.

The secure key storage feature uses Cloudflare’s encrypted infrastructure to manage provider API keys. Applications authenticate to Cloudflare rather than embedding raw provider credentials, improving security and simplifying key rotation.

Analytics and Observability

Comprehensive analytics track requests, tokens, costs, errors, and performance across all providers. The dashboard provides real-time visibility into API usage patterns, helping teams identify optimization opportunities.

Custom metadata tagging enables filtering and analysis by team, feature, or customer. This attribution helps with chargeback models where different business units or customers should be billed for their AI consumption.

When to Choose Cloudflare

Choose Cloudflare AI Gateway when you need global edge deployment with automatic scaling and are already using Cloudflare’s infrastructure. The unified billing simplifies vendor management, while built-in caching and rate limiting provide immediate value without additional configuration.

## Vercel AI Gateway: Seamless Integration for the Vercel Ecosystem

Vercel AI Gateway provides production-ready LLM access with sub-20ms routing latency and automatic failover, designed specifically for teams using Vercel’s deployment platform.

Platform Integration

Built on AI SDK 5, Vercel AI Gateway integrates seamlessly with existing applications using Vercel’s hosting infrastructure. The gateway supports 100+ models across multiple providers with no configuration required.

The OpenAI API compatibility enables migration of existing applications with minimal code changes. Applications already using OpenAI’s SDK work immediately by changing the base URL to Vercel’s gateway endpoint.

Reliability Features

Automatic failover redirects requests during provider outages to available alternatives, ensuring high availability for user-facing applications. The failover logic monitors provider health in real-time, switching traffic without manual intervention.

Sub-20ms routing latency keeps inference times stable regardless of underlying provider. Vercel’s CDN infrastructure, which already powers millions of websites, brings proven reliability to AI workloads.

Pricing and Access Model

Vercel offers a generous free tier with $5 monthly credits for testing all available models without provider restrictions. This removes barriers to experimentation with premium models.

The pay-as-you-go pricing model charges list prices from upstream providers with no markup. Teams can also configure bring-your-own-key (BYOK) to use existing provider credentials at zero cost.

Developer Experience

The gateway prioritizes developer velocity. With one line of code, applications gain access to hundreds of models without managing provider accounts, API keys, or rate limits separately.

Native SDK support for TypeScript, Python, and other languages provides familiar development patterns. The AI SDK 5 abstracts provider differences while preserving advanced capabilities like streaming, tool calling, and structured outputs.

When to Choose Vercel

Select Vercel AI Gateway when you are already deployed on Vercel’s platform and need the fastest path to multi-provider access. The seamless integration with existing infrastructure and generous free tier make it ideal for startups and teams prioritizing development velocity over advanced governance features.

## Kong AI Gateway: Enterprise Governance and Semantic Routing

Kong AI Gateway extends Kong’s mature API management platform to AI traffic, providing enterprise-grade governance, security, and observability for LLM applications.

Enterprise API Management Foundation

Kong brings decades of API management experience to AI infrastructure. Organizations already using Kong for API gateway capabilities can extend their existing deployment to cover AI traffic with consistent policies, authentication, and observability.

The platform supports OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Azure AI, Mistral, and other major providers through a normalized API layer.

Semantic Routing Architecture

Kong’s semantic routing represents the most sophisticated routing capability among the platforms evaluated. The system uses Redis for vector similarity search, enabling intelligent request distribution based on prompt semantics.

At configuration time, administrators define model descriptions explaining each model’s strengths. Kong generates embeddings for these descriptions and stores them in Redis. During request processing, incoming prompts are embedded and compared against model descriptions using vector similarity. Requests automatically route to the model with the highest semantic match.

This enables powerful workflows. A customer support application might configure three models: one fine-tuned for billing questions, another for technical support, and a third for sales inquiries. Kong analyzes incoming questions and routes to the specialized model without explicit classification logic in application code.

Advanced Load Balancing Algorithms

Kong supports six load balancing algorithms including semantic routing, round-robin, least-connections, weighted distribution, latency-based routing, and cost-based routing. This flexibility allows teams to optimize for different objectives across different application areas.

Production deployments often combine multiple strategies. Latency-sensitive user-facing requests use performance-based routing while background batch processing optimizes for cost.

Governance and Security

Token-based rate limiting provides granular control over AI consumption. Instead of limiting requests per second, Kong can limit tokens per day, week, or month at the user, team, or department level. This prevents runaway costs from overly chatty applications or expensive model usage.

PII sanitization and prompt security guardrails prevent sensitive data leakage and prompt injection attacks. The security layer inspects prompts and responses, redacting personally identifiable information before it reaches AI providers or returns to users.

Integration With AI Frameworks

Kong integrates with LangChain, LangGraph, and other agent frameworks, providing governance for complex multi-agent systems. The MCP support enables tool-calling capabilities while maintaining security and compliance controls.

When to Choose Kong

Choose Kong AI Gateway when enterprise governance, compliance, and security are paramount. The semantic routing capabilities excel for applications serving diverse use cases requiring specialized model selection. Organizations already using Kong for API management benefit from unified tooling across traditional and AI APIs.

## LiteLLM: Open-Source Flexibility and Community Innovation

LiteLLM is the open-source powerhouse with 33,000+ GitHub stars, providing maximum flexibility for teams wanting community-driven solutions with complete deployment control.

Comprehensive Provider Support

LiteLLM supports 100+ providers including all major cloud platforms, specialized inference providers, and local deployment options through Ollama, vLLM, and other open-source inference engines.

This breadth makes LiteLLM ideal for teams needing access to emerging providers or specialized models not yet supported by commercial gateways. The active community regularly contributes support for new providers and models.

Dual Deployment Model

LiteLLM offers both a proxy server for centralized gateway deployment and a Python SDK for direct library integration. This flexibility supports different architectural patterns and migration strategies.

Teams can start with the SDK for rapid prototyping, then deploy the proxy server for production workloads requiring centralized authentication, logging, and cost tracking.

Cost Tracking and Budget Management

Virtual keys provide secure access control with per-key budgets and spending limits. The admin dashboard offers real-time visibility into usage patterns, cost attribution, and budget consumption across teams and projects.

Multi-tenant cost tracking enables precise chargeback models. Different business units or customers can have separate virtual keys with isolated budgets and usage reporting.

Observability and Integration

This observability flexibility allows teams to integrate LLM metrics with existing monitoring infrastructure rather than adopting new tooling.

Community and Extensibility

As an open-source project, LiteLLM benefits from community contributions and transparent development. Teams can extend functionality through custom plugins, contribute provider support, or fork the codebase for specialized requirements.

The active GitHub community provides support, shares integration patterns, and collaborates on feature development. This community-driven model accelerates innovation compared to vendor-driven platforms.

When to Choose LiteLLM

Select LiteLLM when you need maximum provider support, deployment flexibility, and open-source transparency. The dual SDK and proxy deployment model supports different architectural requirements. Teams with specialized deployment needs or compliance requirements benefit from complete code visibility and modification capabilities.

## Implementing Effective LLM Routing: Best Practices

Beyond choosing a gateway, successful production deployments require thoughtful implementation strategies.

Start With Observability

Instrument your routing layer from day one. Track request volumes, error rates, latency distributions, and cost attribution across providers and models. Without this baseline, you cannot measure the impact of routing optimizations.

Maxim’s observability platform provides unified visibility across your AI infrastructure. By integrating observability early, teams identify performance bottlenecks and quality issues before they impact users.

Implement Gradual Rollouts

Never switch all traffic to a new model or provider simultaneously. Use percentage-based routing to gradually increase traffic to new endpoints while monitoring quality and performance metrics.

The evaluation workflows in Maxim enable rigorous testing before production rollout. Generate synthetic test cases, route through new models, and measure quality metrics before exposing real users to potential regressions.

Design for Failure

Every provider experiences outages. Your routing layer must handle degraded states gracefully. Implement circuit breakers that prevent cascading failures, fallback chains that maintain availability, and timeout configurations that fail fast rather than blocking indefinitely.

Test failure modes in staging environments. Deliberately inject provider failures and verify that failover logic activates correctly. Agent simulation creates realistic load patterns for stress testing routing infrastructure.

Optimize for Quality, Not Just Cost

The cheapest model rarely delivers the best user experience. Balance cost optimization against quality requirements using data-driven decision making.

Maxim’s evaluation platform enables quantitative quality measurement across routing strategies. Instead of guessing which model performs best, measure accuracy, helpfulness, safety, and other quality dimensions using both automated evaluators and human review workflows.

Monitor Production Quality Continuously

Static evaluation in staging cannot capture all real-world edge cases. Implement continuous quality monitoring in production using sampled evaluation and anomaly detection.

Production observability helps teams detect quality regressions quickl

# Enterprise AI System Design: Inside the AI Architecture of Atlassian & Glean

7 min read

Feb 21, 2026

--

Listen

Share

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI can write code in seconds, but it takes a human architect to design context, security, and truth.

If you asked a developer in 2023 how to build an enterprise AI assistant, the answer was almost a reflex: “Just chunk your documents, push them into a Vector Database, and use RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).”

It sounded so easy. It was the golden hammer for every nail.

Fast forward to 2026, and the industry has realised a harsh truth: Basic RAG is often just a parlour trick. 🪄

It works brilliantly when you want to chat with a single PDF. It fails spectacularly when you deploy it inside a messy, sprawling 10,000-person enterprise.

We have entered a new phase of software engineering. AI agents can now churn out boilerplate code faster than we can type. Today, our real value isn’t just writing syntax , it’s being System Builders.

A System Builder knows that an AI is only as good as the architecture around it. We have to design the guardrails that prevent an AI from hallucinating a deprecated engineering process or, worse, leaking the CEO’s payroll spreadsheet to a summer intern. 😱

To see how the best engineering teams are actually solving this, I tore down the architectures of two of the top Enterprise AI platforms today: Atlassian and Glean.

Both companies came to the same conclusion: To make AI truly useful in the enterprise, vectors aren’t enough. You need a map.

## The Problem: When “Semantic Meaning” Isn’t Enough

Vector databases (the backbone of standard RAG) are incredible at understanding semantic meaning. If you search for “onboarding,” a vector DB knows to retrieve documents about “new hire training” even if the keywords don’t match exactly.

But enterprise software isn’t just about general meaning. It’s about strict relationships, ironclad permissions, and historical context.

- Vectors know “Project ABC” is similar to “Greek Mythology.” They don’t know that User X is the manager of Project ABC.
- Vectors don’t know that a Slack message from yesterday saying “Ignore that memo” supersedes a formal Confluence wiki from 2023.

When you rely solely on vectors, you are essentially asking a brilliant librarian who has read every book to find information, but they have zero knowledge of who checked out the book last or which department owns that shelf.

To solve this, Atlassian and Glean have built their AI platforms on top of Knowledge Graphs which are structured databases that explicitly map the edges (relationships) between nodes (people, documents, tickets, and activities).

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Moving from a messy swamp of vectors to a structured graph of relationships. (Image Source: puppygraph)

Here is the deep-dive into how they engineered it.

## Case Study 1: Atlassian’s Teamwork Graph and “Deep Research” 🕵️‍♂️

As the creator of Jira, Confluence, and Loom, Atlassian designed its AI architecture strictly for Action.

Atlassian’s AI engine, Rovo, isn’t just a chatbot; it is an agentic orchestrator powered by what they call the Teamwork Graph. Instead of looking at tools in isolation, Atlassian engineered a massive graph infrastructure to map how work actually flows.

### 1. The Common Data Model (Unifying the Enterprise)

To build a graph, you first need a standardized language. If Jira speaks “Tickets,” Slack speaks “Messages,” and Google Drive speaks “Files,” the AI gets confused.

Atlassian solved this by engineering a Unified Data Model. Every single item, regardless of where it comes from and is ingested via one of their 100+ connectors and transformed into a standardized Object Type in the graph.

Once normalized, the graph connects these objects using three distinct types of edges (relationships):

- Canonical Relationships: Structural connections (e.g., Epic → contains → Story).
- Activity Relationships: Behavioral data (e.g., User X→ viewed → Confluence Page).
- Inferred Relationships: Machine-learning derived connections (e.g., User Y→ is top collaborator with → User X).

### 2. The Multi-Hop Advantage (Deep Research)

Standard AI struggles with complex, deterministic queries. If you ask a basic LLM, “Why was the billing service delayed last sprint?” it will likely panic and just summarize the first document containing the word “billing.”

Because the Teamwork Graph maps relationships explicitly, Rovo can perform Deep Research. It executes a literal Graph Traversal, hopping from node to node to synthesize a factual timeline.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The “Deep Research” Flow: Instead of searching randomly, the AI traverses the graph edges — from Service, to Ticket, to Code — retracing the actual engineering workflow to find the root cause.

By traversing the graph, the AI isn’t guessing; it’s executing a deterministic search algorithm. It retraces the actual footsteps of the engineering team and cites its sources inline.

## Case Study 2: Glean and the “Invisible Wall” of Security 🛡️

While Atlassian focuses heavily on orchestrating work and action, Glean often called the “Google for the Workplace”, has tackled the single hardest edge case in enterprise AI discovery: Permissions and Consensus.

If you build a naive RAG system, the LLM first searches all company documents to generate an answer, and then you try to filter out what the user shouldn’t see. This is computationally expensive and highly dangerous.

Glean acts as a permission-aware funnel from the very first millisecond, using its Enterprise Graph.

### 1. Hybrid Search & The Math of Consensus (RRF)

Glean recognized that while vectors are great for concepts, they are terrible at finding specific, exact things like error codes (e.g.,`ERR-503`) or internal project acronyms.

To fix this, Glean uses a sophisticated Hybrid Search architecture. Every query runs down two concurrent pipes:

- Dense Retrieval: Using vector embeddings for fuzzy semantic meaning.
- Sparse Retrieval: Using algorithms like BM25 for exact keyword matching.

But how do you merge a vector score (which is a decimal like 0.85) with a BM25 score (which can be a massive number like 14.2)? You can’t just add them.

Enter Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF is a zero-shot algorithm that ignores the raw scores entirely and looks only at the rank position of the document in each list. The formula is beautifully simple:

(Where k is a smoothing constant, usually set to 60).

Why is this brilliant system design? By setting k=60, RRF prioritizes consensus over outliers. If a document ranks #10 in both the Vector search and the Keyword search, it will mathematically beat a document that ranked #1 in Vector but didn’t show up in Keyword at all. It filters out the noise and ensures the AI only reads the highest-quality, agreed-upon context.

### 2. Early Binding and Document-Level Security (ACLs)

Most importantly, Glean uses its Enterprise Graph to enforce Access Control Lists (ACLs) before the LLM ever sees the data.

In system design, this is the debate between “Late Binding” (filtering after search) and “Early Binding” (filtering during search). Glean heavily utilizes Early Binding.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Glean Pipeline: It combines the best of vector and keyword search using RRF, but the crucial step is Early Binding via the ACL Filter. The graph checks permissions before the data ever reaches the LLM.

When a user submits a query, their identity token is checked against the graph. The graph maps the “Permission Edges” between the User node and the Document nodes. If you don’t have a direct relationship to that document’s security group in the graph, the search engine physically cannot retrieve it.

This is security by design. It prevents prompt injections from extracting unauthorised data because the AI is completely blind to files the user doesn’t own.

### The Architect’s Takeaway 🏗️

You can buy an AI model, but you can’t buy context.

If an enterprise’s data is a messy, unconnected swamp of deprecated wikis and orphaned tickets, the most powerful AI in the world will just confidently serve up garbage.

This brings us to a harsh but exciting reality: Coding is no longer the hard part. The era of the System Builder is here. 👑

AI agents can now churn out boilerplate Python scripts and API calls in seconds. But an AI makes a terrible architect. It doesn’t know your business logic, it doesn’t understand your unique edge cases, and it certainly doesn’t know who has permission to view the CEO’s Q4 budget. 😂

Our job as developers has fundamentally evolved. We must be the ones to design the blueprints, combining Hybrid Search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, rigid security protocols, and Unified Data Models, etc. so that when the AI goes to work, it has a map it can actually trust. 🗺️

Anyone can build a cool AI wrapper in a weekend. But building a secure, context-aware production system that a 10,000-person enterprise can actually rely on? That’s what we must train ourselves on!

That is what makes you a System Orchestrator, arguably the most in-demand engineering skill in today’s AI-driven world. 🚀

This article is part of The System Builder Series, where I deconstruct the real-world architectures defining modern engineering, beyond the AI hype. If you found this breakdown helpful, follow me here and on LinkedIn for more architecture deep dives lined up ahead, and drop a comment below on how you are orchestrating systems in your own engineering journey! 👇, or maybe a few claps if you enjoyed the read :)

AI Summary by Glean

- Glean Agents now feature a unified agent builder that combines graph-based and conversational creation, allowing users to seamlessly switch between natural language and step-by-step agent development for greater flexibility and control.
- The introduction of Fast and Thinking modes in the plan & execute step empowers agents to handle more complex, multi-step tasks by optimizing for either speed or depth, with enhanced reasoning capabilities that adapt to real-world ambiguity.
- Enhanced, granular agent permissions and automated agent routing from Glean Assistant provide enterprise-grade governance, enabling targeted sharing, preventing agent sprawl, and ensuring employees receive authoritative answers efficiently.

Every organization is inherently unique—defined by individuals, data, and processes that reflect company goals, competitive dynamics, and market influences. Since these elements are constantly changing, agents cannot remain static; they must adapt dynamically to real-world complexity. As they evolve, robust guardrails are essential to ensure agents access only the right data, interact with the right people, and remain aligned with organizational governance. When implemented with proper control and oversight, agents can transform how businesses operate.

Today, we’re rolling out new capabilities in Glean Agents that make it easier to build and safely scale agents that adapt to real‑world complexity. Our unified agent builder now combines graph-based and conversational builders to deliver a more powerful creation experience. New Fast and Thinking modes in the plan & execution node give agents the ability to solve more complex problems, while users can optimize for speed versus depth in the response. More granular agent permissions and routing capabilities offer admins the tools to better preserve trust, enforce permissions, and prevent sprawl.

## A unified agent builder

After introducing Glean's conversational builder at Glean:LIVE Fall’25, we heard from experienced agent creators that they wanted access to its capabilities, even when creating agents in Glean’s agent builder. Today, we are launching a unified interface that brings our graph-based and conversational builders together. The agent builder now lets you move between natural language agent creation and directly building your agents step by step. You can chat to refactor the entire agent graph—add or remove nodes, edit instructions, learn about individual steps, and even debug. Start in natural language, take manual control where it matters, and scale from simplicity to power-user depth on demand.

Move between natural language agent creation and directly building agents one step at a time.

## Plan & execute steps now include Fast and Thinking modes

We’re upgrading the plan & execute step in Glean’s agent builder to become an increasingly agentic node that takes agency to do the job it was tasked to complete. In the plan & execute steps, agent builders can choose between Fast or Thinking to control how much “effort” an agent puts towards the task—enabling builders to optimize for speed versus depth while leveraging the upgraded reasoning capabilities of Glean’s Agentic Engine 2. This already highly autonomous node now has increased reasoning power and the ability to adapt to the world around it. In both modes, the agent proposes a plan, executes across the enterprise tools you enable, asks for help when clarification is needed, and adaptively plans and re-evaluates as it uncovers new context. This makes it capable of delivering more complete, higher‑quality answers even in ambiguous situations.

With these updates, agents can now handle more complex tasks that require multiple steps or decisions while continuously anchoring to the objective you want to accomplish—like analyzing data or building comprehensive reports that need information across various sources within a single step. In internal testing, both modes improved answer completeness with Thinking mode reaching 94% end‑to‑end answer completeness for our most difficult production use cases.

With Fast and Thinking modes, Glean uses adaptive planning to solve complex problems.

## More granular agent permissions for enterprise-grade control

While the ability to build an agent should be easily accessible for everyone, agent sprawl should be controlled. With more granular agent sharing permissions, admins can control who Agent Creators can share agents with by defining Default Member permissions that limit access to agents based on specific individuals, departments, or an entire company. These Default Member permissions also determine whether each group can publish agents.

We’ve heard from customers that they need this increased granularity as they scale agent rollouts across their company. Not only do these increased permissions help with rollouts, they empower creators to be able to build specialized agents, test them, and share their production-ready agents with targeted groups of relevant individuals. Sensitive and non-relevant agents stay restricted, while high‑quality, broadly applicable agents are easy to find and reuse.

Set permissions to determine who can access agents.

## Agent routing from Assistant

Employees shouldn’t have to hunt for the right agent. With agent routing from Assistant, admins configure rules that automatically send certain questions to specialized agents. Users keep a single entry point—Glean Assistant—and get authoritative answers behind the scenes from the right expert: HR policy questions to your HR agent, access requests to your helpdesk agent, product questions to your roadmap agent, and more. Employees get faster, higher‑quality answers without changing their habits.

Configure rules for when Glean Assistant should route to specific agents.

## Agents that adapt, scale, and improve with you

These capabilities accelerate how agents are securely built and scaled to drive real impact. For agent builders, a unified agent builder speeds up creation, while Thinking mode in the plan & execute step produces sturdier agents that can handle ambiguity. For IT and admins, more granular agent permissions and agent routing from Assistant deliver governance and discoverability at once—so you can scale usage safely without clogging the library or risking oversharing.

Learn more about how to get started with Glean’s agentic features through these resources:

- Getting started with Agent Builder
- How to use Plan and Execute steps
- Managing agent routing capabilities

Check out other details on exciting new Glean features coming your way this week, or sign up for a demo to learn more about Glean!
